claim: Steel-based laminates are hybrid materials consisting of alternating layers of steel sheets and a lighter material, classified as metal–metal or metal–fiber types.
evidence_text: Steel-based laminates are basically hybrid materials containing alternating layers of steel sheets and a lighter material. These can be of metal– metal or metal-fiber types.

claim: Aluminum is the chief alloying element in low-density bulk steels due to its strong density reduction (density of Al = 2.7 g cm−3) and suitable engineering properties.
evidence_text: Considering its strong effect in density reduction (density of Al = 2.7 g cm^{-3}) as well as the engineering aspects such as alloy making and workability, Al has emerged as the chief alloying element in low-density bulk steels.

claim: According to Fig. 1, Al forms a disordered solid solution in Fe up to ~11 wt.%; beyond this, brittle intermetallics D03-Fe3Al and B2-FeAl appear.
evidence_text: As is clear from the Fe-Al phase diagram14 shown in Fig. 1, Al forms disordered solid solution in Fe up to about 11 wt.% of alloying; beyond this amount of Al, various intermetallic phases such as D03-based Fe3Al and B2-based FeAl appear.

claim: Fig. 2 shows that 8 wt.% Al addition causes ~10% density reduction; Fe-Al density drops from 7.8 g cm−3 (0% Al) to 7.05 g cm−3 (8% Al), and Fe-Mn-Al-C (14 wt.% Mn) density falls from 7.1 g cm−3 (6% Al) to 6.95 g cm−3 (8% Al).
evidence_text: Approximately 8 wt.% of Al causes density reduction of 10%. ... | Al content (wt.%) | Fe-Al Density (g.cm⁻³) | Fe-Mn-Al-C Density (g.cm⁻³) | | 0 | 7.8 | - | | 4 | 7.4 | - | | 6 | 7.35 | 7.1 | | 8 | 7.05 | 6.95 | | 10 | 6.8 | 6.75 |

claim: Low-density bulk steels are classified by phase constitution into ferritic (α), austenitic (γ), duplex (α+γ), and triplex (α+γ+κ-carbides) steels.
evidence_text: Depending on the presence of the different phases, low-density steels can be classified into various types such as ferritic (α), austenitic (γ), duplex phase (α+γ), and triplex phase (α+γ+κ-carbides) steels.

claim: According to operative strengthening and ductility-enhancing mechanisms, low-density steels are further classified into IF, bake hardenable, κ-carbide precipitation hardenable, TRIP (including δ-TRIP), TWIP, SIP, and MBIP.
evidence_text: According to the operative strengthening and ductility-enhancing mechanisms, low-density steels can be further classified into following categories. The pure high Al-containing ferritic steels can be interstitial free (IF) and bake hardenable; some of the low-density steels containing austenite are precipitation hardenable (by κ-carbides). Others could involve transformation-induced plasticity (TRIP; including δ-TRIP), twinning-induced plasticity (TWIP), shear band induced plasticity (SIP), or micro band induced plasticity (MBIP)

claim: In duplex low-density steels, an ordering heat treatment can decompose κ-carbides into D03 or B2 phases; when Mn exceeds ~35 wt.%, brittle β-Mn phase forms during aging.
evidence_text: the duplex steels can sometimes contain certain amounts of D03 or B2 phase formed due to decomposition of κ-carbides during an ordering heat treatment. During aging of steels containing κ-carbides, the formation of brittle β-Mn phase has also been reported when the Mn content is very high (>35 wt.%).

claim: Fe-Al ferritic low-density steels are simpler to process and suitable for automotive outer panels but have limited strength and ductility.
evidence_text: The Fe-Al ferritic steels received the least attention in the literature, probably because of their limitations in relation to strength and ductility. However, these alloys are simpler to process and exhibit mechanical properties suitable for automotive outer panel applications.

claim: In ferritic low-density steels, Al content should be restricted to <6.5 wt.% to prevent short-range ordering, which increases brittleness and reduces formability.
evidence_text: it has been suggested that the Al content should be restricted to less than 6.5 wt.%. Short-range ordering, which increases brittleness and therefore is detrimental to formability, can take place with higher Al content (>6.5 wt.%) within the ferritic range.

claim: Carbon content in Al-containing ferritic steels must be minimized to avoid ordered carbides Fe4-yAlyCx (0.8≤y≤1.2; 0≤x≤1) that harm sheet formability; this motivated IF variants with Ti additions to bind interstitial atoms.
evidence_text: It has also been shown that C content in Al-containing ferritic steels should be minimized to avoid the possible formation of ordered carbides, Fe4-yAlyCx (0.8 ≤ y ≤ 1.2; 0 ≤ x ≤ 1), which are detrimental to sheet formability. This has led to the development of high Al-containing low-density IF steels with the addition of a stoichiometric amount of stabilizing elements such as Ti

claim: Small Al additions (<0.04 wt.%) as deoxidizer refine grains via Zener drag of AlN particles, whereas high solute Al leads to coarser grains; free C in the matrix refines grains by solute drag.
evidence_text: Aluminum, when added to steels in small amounts (<0.04 wt.%) as a deoxidizer, causes grain refinement probably through the Zener drag exerted by AlN particles. However, low-density ferritic steels containing high amounts of solute Al exhibit coarser grains... presence of free C in the matrix was shown to refine the grain size, possibly by the solute drag effect of C.

claim: Fig. 3 reports mean grain sizes: Fe-9.7Al-0.04C-0.09Ti 9.7 μm, Fe-8.1Al-0.0015C-0.14Ti 8.1 μm, Fe-6.8Al-0.0035C-0.10Ti 6.8 μm, and conventional IF steel 20 μm.

Bake-hardenable high-Al ferritic steels gain about 80 MPa yield strength after paint baking (2% deformation + 170°C, 20 min); Fig. 4 shows YS increases from 345–418 MPa pre-BH to 425–498 MPa post-BH for several alloys.
evidence_text: It has been reported that about 80 MPa yield strength can be gained through bake hardening in these high Al-containing steels by using a proper alloy design (Fig. 4).

claim: Austenitic low-density steels are Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys with >25 wt.% Mn and atomic fraction of Fe often below 0.5.
evidence_text: Austenitic low-density steels are very high (>25 wt.%) Mn-containing Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys. These steels are concentrated solid solutions of austenite with an atomic fraction of Fe often less than 0.5.

claim: Short-range ordering in austenitic low-density steels persists even after water quenching due to heavy alloying, and κ-carbides precipitate on this short-range order.
evidence_text: Short-range ordering usually takes place in the austenite owing to the thermodynamic factors caused by heavy alloying. Such short-range order exists in steels processed even with a high cooling rate such as water quenching. The κ-carbides precipitate in austenitic steels on the existing short-range order.

claim: Stacking fault energy and κ-carbides dictate the deformation characteristics of austenitic steels, enabling ductility-enhancing mechanisms TWIP, SIP, and MBIP that yield very attractive mechanical properties.
evidence_text: The stacking fault energy of austenite as well as the existence of κ-carbides modifies the deformation characteristics of austenitic steels and give rise to different ductility-enhancing mechanisms, such as TWIP, SIP, and MBIP, yielding very attractive mechanical properties in these alloys

claim: Duplex Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys combine ferrite and austenite; additions of C and Mn enable phase transformation, and ferrite-based duplex grades (with higher Al) reduce density more effectively than austenite-based grades.
evidence_text: Fe-Al low-density steels are basically ferritic in nature. Therefore, to make phase transformation feasible in these steels, austenite stabilizers such as C and Mn are added... The ferrite-based duplex steels are more effective in density reduction than the austenite-based ones because of alloying with higher amount of Al than Mn in the former.

claim: Fig. 5 reveals that in an Fe-6.57Al-3.3Mn-0.18C (wt.%) duplex steel, austenite fraction decreases and ferrite fraction increases with rising temperature in the intercritical region, opposite to standard low-carbon steels.
evidence_text: One interesting feature of duplex Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys, shown in Fig. 5 for a lean alloy duplex steel,5 is that in the intercritical phase field, the austenite (γ) content decreases and the ferrite (α) content increases with increase in temperature, in contrast to the case in standard low-carbon steels.

claim: During continuous cooling or isothermal transformation of duplex steels, ferrite decomposes into grain-boundary fcc (γ21), grain-interior fcc (γ22), and needle-like 18R martensite (γ2′) which exhibits long-period stacking faults and is induced by carbon atoms.
evidence_text: During continuous cooling or isothermal transformation, α decomposes into two types of face-centered cubic (fcc) phases—a grain boundary fcc (γ21) and a grain interior fcc (γ22)—and the needle-like particles of 18R martensite (γ2′). The transformation of bcc ferrite to the needle-like phase, which possesses a long-period stacking fault structure, is believed to be induced by carbon atoms.

claim: During higher-temperature aging, 18R martensite transforms to fcc by introducing pairs of negative dislocations with simultaneous Mn and Al compositional evolution; prolonged aging produces a dodecahedron α-Mn phase with cubic-on-cubic orientation relationship.
evidence_text: The 18R martensite, during higher temperature aging, transforms to an fcc structure by introducing pairs of negative dislocations (with Burgers vector equal to negative direction of the lattice invariant shear)46 with concomitant compositional evolution of Mn and Al. In addition, the formation of dodecahedron α-Mn phase, which is a solid solution of Fe, Mn and Al, also occurs after prolonged aging of 18R martensite with a cubic-on-cubic matrix-precipitate orientation relationship.

claim: Triplex low-density steels (α+γ+κ-carbide) are produced by solution annealing in the two- or three-phase field, quenching, and aging, or by continuous cooling from the single-phase ferritic domain.
evidence_text: Depending on the chemistry of steels, the basic process route of producing triplex steels involves a solution annealing usually in a two-phase (α+γ) or three-phase (α+γ+κ-carbide) field and quenching followed by an aging step5,32 or a continuous cooling from the single-phase ferritic domain.

claim: In triplex steels, κ-carbides form from C-enriched austenite regions (likely via spinodal decomposition during quenching) and further transformations yield a modulated structure (Fig. 6).
evidence_text: The κ-carbides grow from C-enriched areas of the austenitic matrix formed most probably via spinodal decomposition during quenching and precipitate. ... If this transformation is partially complete, then κ-carbides precipitate in austenite giving rise to a modulated structure.

claim: Fig. 6 schematically depicts the phase transformation sequence in lean Mn Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys: α → γ nucleation → κ-carbide ring at α/γ interface → fragmentation/globularization → total or partial γ decomposition to α+κ or modulated structure.
evidence_text: As shown schematically in Fig. 6, for low Mn-containing steels during continuous cooling, initially austenite forms from ferrite, and then κ-carbides form at the interphase boundaries of austenite and ferrite. At lower temperatures, austenite decomposes to ferrite and κ-carbide through a eutectoid reaction. If this transformation is partially complete, then κ-carbides precipitate in austenite giving rise to a modulated structure.

claim: In Fe-(23–32)Mn-(2–10)Al-(0.4–1)C (wt.%) alloys, intragranular κ-carbide cannot precipitate when Al < 6.2 and C < 1 wt.%, whereas intergranular κ-carbide requires Al > 5.5 and C > 0.67 wt.%.
evidence_text: Investigations on a series of alloys with compositions, Fe-(23-32)Mn-(2-10)Al-(0.4-1)C (wt.%) led to the conclusion that intragranular jcarbide cannot precipitate in these alloys only when Al and C contents are lower than about 6.2 and 1 wt.%, respectively. In contrast, intergranular jcarbide precipitation in these alloys is possible if Al and C contents are larger than 5.5 and 0.67 wt.% respectively.

claim: The approximate composition boundary for intragranular κ-carbide formation in Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys is given by the equation 0.098Al + 0.208C = 1 - 0.0054Mn (wt.%).
evidence_text: Furthermore, the following approximate composition boundary for formation of intragranular precipitates of j-carbide was derived, for the alloys with above composition range, based on calculation of a lower critical lattice constant of austenite below which j-carbide would not form: 0.098Al + 0.208C = 1 - 0.0054Mn.

claim: In ferritic Fe-Al-C alloys, κ-carbide is Mn-free (FeAlC0.5) and a potent strengthener; increasing C content increases FeAlC0.5 particles and improves strengthening, but ordered Fe3Al precipitates also strengthen ferrite while negatively impacting ductility.
evidence_text: In ferritic Fe-Al-C alloys, j-carbide is basically Mn-free (FeAlC0.5) and a potent strengthener of ferrite. Increasing the C content in ferritic steels increases the amount of FeAlC0.5 particles, and therefore the strengthening response improves. Furthermore, the ferrite in Fe-Al-C alloys can also be strengthened significantly by the presence of small amount of precipitate of the ordered Fe3Al phase ... However, these strengthening phases have a negative impact on ductility of the material.

claim: The TRIP effect occurs in lean Mn-containing duplex steels (e.g., Fe-3.5Mn-5.8Al-0.35C wt.%) when the stacking fault energy of austenite is below ~20 mJ m⁻², and its effectiveness depends on intercritical annealing temperature.
evidence_text: Strain-induced displace transformation of austenite to body-centered tetragonal martensite occurs when the stacking fault energy of austenite is less than about 20 mJ m^{-2}; ... The existence of a TRIP effect was reported in lean Mn-containing duplex steels (e.g., Fe-3.5Mn-5.8Al-0.35C by wt.%). The TRIP effect was also found to be dependent on the annealing temperature in the intercritical phase field because it controls the partitioning of elements in austenite and ferrite, and thereby the stability of austenite.

claim: δ-TRIP steels retain a large amount of δ-ferrite from solidification and use 3–5 wt.% Al to suppress cementite and form an adherent fayalite scale during hot rolling, resulting in a density reduction up to ~5%.
evidence_text: In these steels, a large amount of dferrite is retained in the microstructure from the solidification process at all temperatures. ... The latter benefit arises because the design of d-TRIP steels uses an addition of somewhat high amount of Al (3–5 wt.%), ... That Al is added in d-TRIP steels in relatively high amount results in density reduction of the steel up to about 5%.

claim: The SIP effect in Fe-(26–28)Mn-(11–12)Al-(0.9–1.15)C (wt.%) triplex steels arises from uniformly arranged shear bands on {111} planes induced by regular distribution of coherent nanosized κ-carbides, enabled by very stable austenite with a Gibbs energy of 1755 J mol⁻¹ and stacking fault energy of 110 mJ m⁻² (Fig. 8).
evidence_text: The SIP effect, which is based on homogeneous shear deformation, has been reported in triplex steels with approximate compositions of Fe-(26–28)Mn-(11–12)Al-(0.9–1.15)C (wt.%). A regular distribution of nanosized j-carbides that are coherent in austenite matrix give rise to uniformly arranged shear bands on {111} planes as shown in Fig. 8. ... very stable austenite that possesses high positive Gibbs energy for austenite to martensitic transformation (1755 J mol-1) and relatively high stacking fault energy (110 mJ m-2).

claim: The MBIP effect is postulated as the governing ductility-enhancing mechanism in Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.8C (wt.%) austenitic steel, where microbands nucleate on domain boundaries and progressively increase dislocation density, producing a higher strain-hardening rate and larger elongation.
evidence_text: MBIP was postulated to be the governing ductility-enhancing mechanism in an Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.8C (wt.%) austenitic steel. Microbands, consisting of geometrically necessary dislocations, nucleate on domain boundaries and grow progressively with strain causing enhanced dislocation density. A higher strain-hardening rate is obviously linked with the increased dislocation density, effecting a larger elongation.

claim: Austenite-containing duplex and triplex low-density steels exhibit superior strength–ductility balance and greater density reduction compared to ferritic low-density steels, attributed to multiple strengthening and ductility-enhancing mechanisms and higher alloying content (Fig. 9).
evidence_text: As can be seen, steels containing austenite (duplex and triplex steels) are superior in strength–ductility balance as well as in terms of density reduction than ferritic steels because in the former category of steels (I) different strength and ductility-enhancing mechanisms are operative … and (II) they have a higher alloying content (Al and Mn).

claim: In ferritic low-density steels, solid-solution strengthening by Al contributes approximately 40 MPa per wt.% Al.
evidence_text: The strength in ferritic alloys originates mainly from the solid-solution strengthening by Al (≈40 MPa/wt.%Al).

claim: To avoid preordering and obtain satisfactory ductility, the Al content in ferritic low-density steels should be kept below 6.5 wt.%.
evidence_text: to obtain satisfactory ductility by avoiding preordering, the Al content in ferritic steels needs to be below 6.5 wt.%.

claim: Free carbon in ferritic low-density steels causes discontinuous yielding (Fig. 10), which can produce surface stretcher marks after forming, and therefore carbon should be stabilized.
evidence_text: The presence of free C in ferritic steels can cause discontinuous yielding in the tensile curve (Fig. 10), which can give rise to surface stretcher marks on sheets after forming, and therefore carbon should preferably be stabilized.

claim: Ferritic low-density steels show lower stretching and deep drawing behaviors than conventional IF steels due to weakening of γ-texture and a decrease of strain-hardening exponent with Al addition.
evidence_text: The stretching and deep drawing behaviors of ferritic steels were reported to be lower than conventional IF steels because of the weakening of γtexture and a decrease of strain-hardening exponent of ferrite matrix with Al-addition.

claim: Austenite-containing low-density steels exhibit high strain hardening rate as a key mechanical property; a typical MBIP steel (Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.8C) shows very high strain-hardening rate in its true stress–true strain curve (Fig. 11).
evidence_text: In low-density steels containing austenite, … the strain hardening rate is a key mechanical property of these steels affecting strength and ductility. As an example, the true stress–true strain curve and the corresponding strain -hardening rate as a function of true strain are shown in Fig. 11 for an MBIP steel.

claim: Austenitic and duplex low-density steels exhibit similar tensile strength but austenitic grades are highly ductile; compared to DP800 and TRIP780, these low-density steels show far greater ductility and stretchability (Tables I and II).
evidence_text: The austenitic and duplex (ferrite or austenite based) low-density steels exhibit similar levels of tensile strength; however, the austenitic low-density steels are highly ductile… In comparison with the commonly used steel grades with even slightly lower strength such as DP800 and TRIP780, these low-density steels are far more ductile and stretchable (Tables I and II).

claim: Al addition to steel decreases Young's modulus (Fig. 12), which can negatively affect the stiffness of automotive components.
evidence_text: a negative effect of Al addition to steel is that it causes the Young’s modulus of steels to decrease, as shown in Fig. 12. This can influence the application of low-density steels in automotives in a negative manner because the Young’s modulus of a material controls the stiffness directly.

claim: A coiling temperature of 650°C was used for ultralow C or interstitial-free Fe-Al-(C) alloys, 400°C for lean Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, and room temperature for rich Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, to improve processability and formability.
evidence_text: A fairly high coiling temperature (650°C) was used for ultralow C or interstitial-free type Fe-Al-(C) alloys, whereas a low coiling temperature (400°C) and room-temperature coiling were employed for lean and rich alloy Fe-Mn-Al-C steels, respectively.

claim: Al raises the ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT) in steels, causing ferritic alloys with Al content greater than 8 wt.% to crack during cold rolling, therefore warm rolling is applied.
evidence_text: As Al raises the ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT) in steels, higher Al-containing ferritic alloys crack during cold rolling, and therefore warm rolling has been applied to reduce the gauge of these steels with Al content greater than 8 wt.%.

claim: Young's modulus of low-density steels decreases with increasing Al content: from 205 GPa at 0 wt.% Al to 163 GPa at 6 wt.% Al (Rana et al.), 158 GPa at 8 wt.% Al, and 153 GPa at 10 wt.% Al; Frommeyer et al. reported 190 GPa at 6 wt.% Al and 178 GPa at 8 wt.% Al.
evidence_text: | Al content (wt.%) | Young's modulus (GPa) - Rana et al. [6, 20] | Young's modulus (GPa) - Frommeyer et al. [18] | | 0 | 205 | - | | 6 | 163 | 190 | | 8 | 158 | 178 | | 10 | 153 | - |

claim: For ferritic low-density alloys, commercial continuous annealing facilities operate at 800–850°C, which has been adopted in laboratory studies; recrystallization and grain growth considerations limit the usable annealing temperature range.
evidence_text: the temperatures used for operation of commercial continuous annealing facilities (e.g. 800–850°C) have been used in laboratory studies. Furthermore, considerations of recrystallization and grain growth can also put a limit on the practical usable range of annealing temperatures for these steels.

claim: Fe-Mn-Al-C multiphase steels are sometimes given an aging treatment at 400–450°C after continuous annealing to form or change the precipitate state of carbides, which improves mechanical properties.
evidence_text: The Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys are sometimes given an aging treatment after continuous annealing to form or change the precipitate state of carbides. This low-temperature (400–450°C) step can be easily performed in the overaging section that exists in most continuous annealing lines of modern sheet steel processing facilities.

claim: The ferritic IF low-density steel Fe-6.8Al-0.0035C-0.1Ti in cold-rolled and annealed condition exhibits yield strength 342 MPa, ultimate tensile strength 465 MPa, uniform elongation 18.5%, total elongation 31.1%, strain hardening exponent n=0.17, and normal anisotropy r̄=1.37.
evidence_text: Ferritic (IF) | Fe-6.8Al-0.0035C-0.1Ti | Cold rolled and annealed | 342 | 465 | 18.5 | 31.1 | 0.17 | 1.37 | 20

claim: The austenitic low-density steel Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1.2C in solutionized and aged condition shows yield strength 540 MPa, ultimate tensile strength 895 MPa, uniform elongation 65%, total elongation 73.6%, and strain hardening exponent n=0.29.
evidence_text: Austenitic | Fe-30.5Mn-8Al-1.2C | Solutionized and aged | 540 | 895 | 65 | 73.6 | 0.29 | - | 58

claim: The triplex low-density steel Fe-5.8Al-3.5Mn-0.35C in annealed and aged condition has yield strength 667 MPa, ultimate tensile strength 794 MPa, total elongation 34%, and strain hardening exponent n=0.16.
evidence_text: Triplex | Fe-5.8Al-3.5Mn-0.35C | Annealed and aged | 667 | 794 | - | 34.0 | 0.16 | - | 32

claim: The conventional dual-phase steel DP800 exhibits yield strength 469 MPa, ultimate tensile strength 834 MPa, uniform elongation 12.3%, total elongation 17.9%, and strain hardening exponent n=0.13.
Ferritic low-density steels are annealed at temperatures 800–850°C in laboratory studies, matching commercial continuous annealing facility temperatures.
evidence_text: the temperatures used for operation of commercial continuous annealing facilities (e.g. 800–850°C) have been used in laboratory studies.6,20

claim: Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys are given a low-temperature aging treatment at 400–450°C after continuous annealing to modify carbide precipitates and improve mechanical properties.
evidence_text: The Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys are sometimes given an aging treatment after continuous annealing to form or change the precipitate state of carbides.5,32 This low-temperature (400–450°C) step can be easily performed in the overaging section... The overaging has been reported to bring about improvements in mechanical properties.5

claim: In a ferrite-dominated duplex low-density steel, the recrystallization stop temperature of austenite is much higher than that of ferrite, and austenite recrystallization starts only at approximately 1200°C after recovery of ferrite.
evidence_text: The recrystallization stop temperature of austenite was found to be much higher than that of ferrite in a ferrite-dominated duplex steel... The recrystallization does not initiate in austenite until at a very high temperature (~1200°C) where recovery gets completed.27

claim: The low-density ferrite-based duplex steel Fe-6.57Al-3.3Mn (wt.%) has a density of 7.27 g cm−3 and a Young's modulus of 181 GPa, 14% lower than traditional steels.
evidence_text: low-density ferrite-based duplex steel containing 6.57Al-3.3Mn by wt.%.5 ... density of 7.27 g cm-3 and a Young's modulus of 181 GPa, which is about 14% lower than that of traditional steels.5

claim: HSLA steels, ferrite-based, were assumed to have a Young's modulus of 210 GPa and a density of 7.85 g cm−3 in the CAE stiffness study.
evidence_text: The Young's modulus and density of HSLA steels, which are basically ferrite-based steels, were assumed to be 210 GPa and 7.85 g cm-3 , respectively.

claim: In a CAE study on a Volkswagen Golf V BIW, replacing HSLA steels with the Fe-6.57Al-3.3Mn duplex steel while maintaining torsional stiffness resulted in a predicted 14.5% BIW weight reduction; shell thickness distributions are shown in Fig. 14.
evidence_text: The shell thicknesses of different BIW parts, made of HSLA and low-density steels providing the same torsional stiffness values for the whole BIW are shown in Fig. 14. ... a weight reduction of 14.5% of the BIW was predicted for replacing all HSLA steel sheets by the considered low-density steel sheets.5

claim: Young's modulus directly influences stiffness, so the reduced modulus of low-density steels is critical for stiffness-controlled automotive components.
evidence_text: Because Young's modulus directly influences the stiffness,5,20 this stiffness-related study involving a low-density steel with reduced Young's modulus can be considered to be very critical.

claim: Future research on low-density steels should focus on thermodynamics, heat treatment science of duplex alloys, deformation mechanisms in multiphase alloys, strategies to improve Young's modulus and formability, and overcoming processing challenges.
evidence_text: The research focus of these steels should concentrate on understanding the thermodynamics of these alloys, the physical metallurgy of heat treatments (annealing and overaging) of duplex alloys, clarifying the fundamentals of the deformation mechanisms operating in multiphase alloys, the alloying and process strategies to improve the Young's modulus, and formability particularly in ferritic types, along with overcoming the processing challenges...
